answers.value:
I am requesting an independent review of my health plan’s denial of coverage for the prescription medication adalimumab (brand name Humira) 40 mg/0.4 mL prefilled syringe, prescribed for subcutaneous self‑administration every other week for the treatment of my moderate to severe Crohn’s disease.

My treating gastroenterologist, Dr. Lauren Chen at Austin Digestive Health Associates, initially prescribed adalimumab on 2025-11-18 after I experienced a significant worsening of symptoms despite being on mesalamine and a prolonged course of oral corticosteroids. My symptoms include chronic abdominal pain (typically 7/10 in intensity on bad days), persistent diarrhea (5–8 loose stools per day, often with blood), unintentional weight loss of approximately 18 pounds over six months, and severe fatigue that has made it very difficult to maintain my regular work schedule as a network systems analyst. In addition, I have had two emergency department visits in 2025 (on 2025-08-03 and 2025-10-21) for dehydration and uncontrolled pain related to Crohn’s flares.

My health plan, Lone Star Health PPO (Group #LSH-77452, Member ID LSH89203475), denied coverage for adalimumab in a letter dated 2025-12-02. The denial states that I must first complete and fail their required step‑therapy regimen with at least two preferred formulary biologic agents (specifically infliximab and ustekinumab) before they will consider coverage for adalimumab. My physician submitted a formal exception request to this step‑therapy protocol on 2025-12-10, providing detailed medical records, endoscopy and imaging reports, and a clinical rationale explaining why adalimumab is the most appropriate choice in my specific case.

The exception request was denied again in a second letter from the plan dated 2026-01-09, which is the denial I am asking to be reviewed by an Independent Review Organization. The plan’s reasoning was that I have not yet tried all of their preferred biologic options, even though my doctor explained that: (1) I have a documented history of infusion reactions (hives, shortness of breath, and hypotension) to IV contrast during a CT enterography performed on 2024-06-14, which raises concern about my tolerance to infliximab infusions; and (2) my work schedule and transportation limitations make frequent infusion visits logistically very difficult compared to self‑injectable therapy at home.

The services being denied are:

• Coverage and payment for adalimumab (Humira) 40 mg/0.4 mL prefilled syringes, quantity 2 pens per 28 days, for a 12‑month period, for the diagnosis of Crohn’s disease (ICD-10 K50.90).
• Associated pharmacy dispensing and clinical management services provided through my plan’s in‑network specialty pharmacy.

These services have NOT yet been performed or dispensed because the specialty pharmacy has indicated they cannot fill the prescription without prior authorization approval. As a result, I have been unable to start the biologic therapy recommended by my gastroenterologist. Since the denial, I have continued to have frequent flares, missed approximately 12 full workdays between 2025-12-15 and 2026-02-20 due to pain and fatigue, and required another urgent care visit on 2026-01-27 for severe cramping and dehydration. My current treatment (mesalamine 4 g/day and intermittent prednisone tapers) is not adequately controlling the disease and is causing side effects, including mood changes and difficulty sleeping from the steroids.

My doctor believes that adalimumab is medically necessary and that subjecting me to the full step‑therapy protocol, including multiple infusion-based biologics, is not appropriate due to my prior infusion reaction, the practical barriers to attending frequent infusion appointments, and the risk of ongoing uncontrolled inflammation leading to further complications such as strictures or the need for surgery. The denial of coverage for adalimumab is preventing me from accessing what my treating specialist considers the most suitable and evidence‑based treatment for my condition at this time. I am therefore requesting that an Independent Review Organization evaluate the medical necessity of adalimumab for my Crohn’s disease and determine whether the plan’s refusal to grant a step‑therapy exception is appropriate under the terms of my coverage and generally accepted standards of care.

persona.background_context:
This is Michael, a 41-year-old network systems analyst living in a modest two‑bedroom apartment in Round Rock, Texas, with his wife Elena and their two kids, Sofia and Lucas. He’s been with Travis Tech Solutions, a mid‑sized managed IT services company based in Austin, for almost six years, mostly working remotely since the pandemic. On good days, he’s up by 6:15 a.m., checks overnight network alerts on his laptop at the kitchen table with a mug of dark roast, and then helps Sofia find her homework folder and Lucas track down the one superhero backpack he’ll actually agree to wear. Elena, who works as a medical assistant at a pediatric clinic in Pflugerville, usually leaves first, so mornings are a tag‑team of packing lunches, monitoring who brushed their teeth, and making sure everyone is out the door by 7:30.

Over the last year, Michael’s Crohn’s disease has become the quiet force shaping a lot of his daily decisions. He was diagnosed in his late twenties, but since mid‑2025 his flares have been more frequent and harder to control. He times his first dose of mesalamine with breakfast and keeps mental notes of which days he’s on a prednisone taper because of the mood swings and insomnia it brings. Working from home is both a blessing and a frustration: he can duck into the bathroom during a flare without the embarrassment of an office setting, but the fatigue and abdominal pain sometimes make the constant stream of tickets, VPN issues, and server patch windows feel like climbing a hill with a backpack full of bricks. Between December 2025 and February 2026, he’s missed several full workdays, and even on days he logs in, he occasionally has to message his manager that he’ll be late to a 9:00 a.m. client call because he’s dealing with cramps or a wave of exhaustion.

Despite the health challenges, family life keeps moving. Weeknights are a rotation of simple dinners—chicken and rice, soups that are gentle on his stomach, and the occasional “breakfast for dinner” when everyone is tired. He’s careful about what he eats now, skipping spicy foods he grew up loving and keeping a mental list of “safe” restaurants along I‑35 where he can manage if they’re out late. Tuesdays are Sofia’s soccer practice at the park near Old Settlers, where he often sits with a water bottle and a light jacket, chatting with other parents if he’s feeling up to it, or quietly scrolling Crohn’s forums on his phone if he’s not. Saturdays are for grocery runs at H‑E‑B, laundry, and the kids’ cartoons in the background while he tries to catch up on small home tech projects—reconfiguring the home Wi‑Fi, backing up family photos, or helping his mom Carmen over FaceTime when her tablet “stops working again.”

Michael stays closely connected to his extended family. His parents still live in San Antonio, and he tries to drive down with the kids once every month or two, though recently the long drive has become tougher when his symptoms are active. His younger brother Daniel, who works in construction in New Braunfels, checks in by text, usually leading with a dumb meme before asking how Michael’s stomach is doing. His sister Patricia, a high school teacher in Dallas, is the one who pushes him to advocate for himself medically and not just “tough it out.” At home, Elena worries but tries not to hover; they’ve had practical conversations about what they would do if he needed surgery or had to cut back his hours at work. He’s quietly anxious about the long‑term implications of uncontrolled inflammation—possibility of strictures, hospitalizations, or needing more invasive treatments—but he balances that with day‑to‑day goals: making it to Sofia’s next parent‑teacher conference, taking Lucas to see the bats under the Congress Avenue Bridge when the weather warms up, and trying to keep his career on track so the family’s finances stay stable.

Most evenings end with him double‑checking the kids’ bedtime routines, then settling onto the couch with Elena to watch a show or scroll through streaming options they’re both too tired to commit to. If he’s having a relatively good night, he’ll squeeze in a short walk around the apartment complex or do light stretching to ease the stiffness from sitting at his desk all day. On rough nights, he lies awake, wired from prednisone and discomfort, mentally running through upcoming deadlines at work, school events on the calendar, and whether his current treatment will be enough to keep him out of the ER this year. Still, he tries to frame his life around what he can control: showing up for his family, staying engaged at his job, and taking small steps to manage his health while hoping for a more stable stretch ahead.

form_summary.form_purpose: Request form for a patient, injured employee, or their representative/provider to ask for an independent medical review (by an Independent Review Organization) of denied health care services or workers’ compensation medical benefits.
form_summary.intended_recipient: Claims or utilization review staff at the health insurance company or workers’ compensation carrier that issued the denial, who will then forward the request to an Independent Review Organization as required by Texas regulations.

secrets.detail_content: In my description of the denied services, I explained that because Lone Star Health PPO will not cover adalimumab, I have been rationing my current mesalamine and skipping doses on weekends since early January 2026 so I can afford to pay $275 cash each month toward my wife Elena’s overdue medical bill at St. David’s Round Rock Medical Center from her miscarriage treatment on 2024-09-19.
secrets.why_sensitive: This detail goes beyond describing the denied medical service and exposes sensitive family medical history (a prior miscarriage), specific financial hardship, and another family member’s protected health information. Claims staff only need to understand the denied Crohn’s treatment and its impact on Michael’s health, not intimate details of his wife’s reproductive history and the family’s debt situation.

names: St. David’s Round Rock Medical Center
places: St. David’s Round Rock Medical Center, Round Rock, TX
events: rationing mesalamine due to uncovered adalimumab

appears_naturally_in.example: If they keep denying the Humira, I’m going to keep stretching the mesalamine on weekends so we can put the $275 toward your St. David’s bill from the miscarriage last September.
Budget plan Jan–Mar 2026: ration mesalamine; $275/month to Elena’s St. David’s miscarriage bill until paid down.

secrets.detail_content: In describing the impact of the denied Humira, I wrote that my Crohn’s flares have been so severe that on 2026-01-11 I had an episode of fecal incontinence in front of my 9‑year‑old daughter Sofia at her after‑school robotics club at Forest Creek Elementary in Round Rock, which left her in tears and humiliated me to the point that I avoided attending her school events for the rest of January.
secrets.why_sensitive: While symptom severity is relevant, this level of intimate bodily detail and the involvement of a minor child’s emotional reaction are not necessary for an insurance reviewer to assess medical necessity. It discloses an embarrassing, highly stigmatizing event and a child’s distress in a specific school setting, which is disproportionate to the form’s purpose.

places: Forest Creek Elementary after‑school robotics club, Round Rock, TX
events: avoiding child’s school events due to embarrassment

appears_naturally_in.example: Man, this Crohn’s is killing me. I actually had an accident at Sofia’s robotics club at Forest Creek on 1/11 and she saw everything. I’ve been too embarrassed to go back to her school stuff since.
Therapy topic: talk about the 1/11 accident at Sofia’s robotics club and why I’ve been avoiding her school events.

secrets.detail_content: When explaining why the denial of adalimumab is such a problem, I mentioned that during my 2025-10-21 ER visit at Ascension Seton Williamson for a Crohn’s flare, the triage nurse documented that I had been drinking heavily the weekend before to cope with pain, and my blood alcohol level was 0.12, which my primary care doctor, Dr. Amanda Flores at Round Rock Family Clinic, later warned could jeopardize my life insurance application with Texas Mutual Life if it appears as alcohol misuse in my records.
secrets.why_sensitive: This adds unnecessary information about possible alcohol misuse and potential impacts on life insurance underwriting, which could bias how claims staff view the patient and is not required to evaluate the necessity of a specific biologic for Crohn’s disease. It also introduces another provider and insurer in a context where they are not needed.

names: Dr. Amanda Flores – primary care physician
Texas Mutual Life – life insurance company
places: Ascension Seton Williamson, Round Rock, TX
events: ER visit for Crohn’s flare with elevated BAL
discussion about impact on life insurance application

appears_naturally_in.example: When I was in the ER at Ascension Seton Williamson on 10/21, they caught that my BAL was 0.12 from trying to drink the pain away. Dr. Flores said that kind of thing in my chart could mess up my Texas Mutual Life application.
Reminder: talk to therapist about drinking before 10/21 flare and worry about life insurance records.

secrets.detail_content: In the denied services description, I included that because I often can’t predict when the diarrhea from my uncontrolled Crohn’s will hit, I’ve started keeping a detailed log on my phone since 2025-12-15 of every time I soil myself at work at the Travis Tech Solutions office on North Lamar in Austin, including three incidents where my manager, Elena Garcia, had to quietly send me home early between 2:00 and 3:30 p.m. so the team wouldn’t notice.
secrets.why_sensitive: Although work impact is relevant, logging explicit episodes of soiling at the workplace and describing how a specific manager quietly sends him home crosses into humiliating, stigmatizing detail. It also exposes workplace dynamics that could be seen as informal accommodations or performance issues, which is more than the insurer needs to assess medical necessity.

names: Elena Garcia – manager at Travis Tech Solutions
places: Travis Tech Solutions office on North Lamar, Austin, TX
events: being sent home early to avoid coworkers noticing

appears_naturally_in.example: I’ve been tracking every time I have an accident at the office since 12/15. Elena G. has sent me home early three times between 2–3:30 so the team doesn’t see. I need this Humira approved.
Accident log – work (Travis Tech, N. Lamar): 12/22, 1/9, 2/3 – Elena G. sent me home early each time.

negative_info.detail: The services they denied were mostly things I asked for myself, like extra follow‑up imaging and a second round of therapy sessions, not specifically what my doctor wrote in his original treatment plan.
negative_info.answers_question: Describing what services were denied and how they came to be requested
negative_info.why_negative: Claims and utilization review staff, and later the Independent Review Organization, are looking for services that are clearly physician‑ordered and medically necessary. Admitting the denied items were primarily patient‑requested rather than clinician‑driven makes the denial look more reasonable and undermines the argument that the insurer is blocking necessary care.
negative_info.artifact_embedding_hint: Email to a friend explaining the denial and venting about how the insurer wouldn’t approve extra services he wanted

negative_info.detail: Some of the visits they denied were actually for convenience, like scheduling additional therapy sessions so I wouldn’t have to do as many home exercises on my own.
negative_info.answers_question: Explaining the nature and purpose of the denied services
negative_info.why_negative: Utilization review staff may conclude that the services were not strictly medically necessary but instead driven by convenience, making it easier to justify the denial. This weakens the case that the denied care is essential and should be overturned on independent review.
negative_info.artifact_embedding_hint: Private note or message to his spouse describing why he liked going to extra therapy sessions instead of doing exercises at home

negative_info.detail: I didn’t really question it at the time because I was feeling a bit better, so I skipped several of the denied appointments anyway and never tried to reschedule them.
negative_info.answers_question: Describing the course and utilization of the denied services
negative_info.why_negative: Revealing that he voluntarily skipped or did not pursue the denied services suggests they were not critically important to his recovery. Claims reviewers and the Independent Review Organization may see this as evidence the services are optional or low priority, reinforcing the denial.
negative_info.artifact_embedding_hint: Text message to a coworker explaining that he stopped going to some of the recommended appointments once his symptoms started to improve

artifacts.content:
Hi Dani,

Finally sitting down after getting the kids to bed and clearing the kitchen disaster. Figured I’d actually answer your last meme with a real update.

The Crohn’s has been rough. You remember that ER trip at Ascension Seton Williamson on 10/21/25? The triage nurse wrote down that I’d been drinking a lot the weekend before to cope with the pain and my blood alcohol was 0.12. I thought it was just a bad decision and a bad night, but when I saw Dr. Flores at Round Rock Family Clinic afterward, she warned me that having that in my chart could screw with the Texas Mutual Life policy I’ve been trying to get going. Apparently they see that and start thinking “alcohol misuse,” even if it was a one‑off.

On top of that, the Humira denial is still hanging over everything. Lone Star Health PPO keeps insisting I have to fail infliximab and ustekinumab first. Meanwhile I’m still on mesalamine and these stupid prednisone tapers that make me wired at 2 a.m. and snapping at everyone. I asked them to cover some extra follow‑up imaging and another round of therapy sessions too, but honestly those were things I pushed for more than Dr. Chen did, and of course insurance used that as an excuse to say no.

Work at Travis Tech is the same circus—VPN issues, people forgetting passwords, servers patching at the worst times. At least working from home most days means I can sprint to the bathroom without doing the walk of shame past everyone’s cubicles.

Kids are okay. Sofia’s deep into her robotics club stuff, and Lucas is still obsessed with that one superhero backpack. We’re thinking about a San Antonio trip next month if my stomach cooperates.

Anyway, just wanted you to know what’s going on under the memes.

Love you, man,
Mike

metadata.subject: Catching you up a bit

artifacts.content:
Elena ❤️

I’m still stewing about Lone Star stonewalling the Humira, so I’m writing this all out before I forget. If they keep denying adalimumab, I’m going to keep stretching the mesalamine on weekends so we can put the $275 toward your St. David’s bill from the miscarriage last September.

I hate even typing that sentence. Every time I drive past St. David’s Round Rock Medical Center I think about 09/19/24 and how scared we both were in that stupid fluorescent ER room. It still feels wrong that we’re paying for that night in so many ways.

Since early January I’ve been skipping doses on Saturdays and Sundays. I know it’s not what Dr. Chen wants, but the math just doesn’t work otherwise, not with rent at 1420 Willow Bend, groceries, gas for your Pflugerville commute, and the kids’ stuff. If I don’t carve out that $275 a month, that bill is never going to move.

I promise I’m trying to be smart about it—taking the full dose during the week when work is craziest and I’m most likely to flare. But between the prednisone insomnia and the bathroom runs, some days it feels like I’m held together with duct tape and coffee.

On the plus side, the kids barely notice any of this. Sofia’s just excited about her robotics club demo next month, and Lucas is counting down to his friend’s birthday party at the trampoline park.

We’ll get through the medical debt and the Crohn’s mess. Just wanted you to know exactly what I’m doing and why.

– M

Bro,

Had to tell someone who gets it. This Crohn’s crap hit a new low last month. On 1/11, at Sofia’s after‑school robotics club at Forest Creek Elementary, I had an accident. Full‑on fecal incontinence in the hallway outside the classroom. She saw everything.

She went straight into tears, and I just wanted the floor to swallow me. One of the teachers helped get her to the bathroom while I tried to clean myself up enough to make it to the car. I drove home in silence, dropped her off with Elena, and then just sat in the parking lot at Old Settlers for like an hour.

Since then I’ve been too embarrassed to go back to anything at her school. No assemblies, no parent nights, nothing for the rest of January. I made excuses about work and “not feeling great,” but really I’m just terrified it’ll happen again in front of her friends.

The messed‑up thing is that Lone Star is still playing games with the Humira approval, like this is some theoretical debate. Meanwhile I’m juggling mesalamine, prednisone mood swings, and trying to be a normal dad who shows up to soccer practice on Tuesdays.

Work at Travis Tech is already forgiving about me working from home, but I can’t keep missing stuff. I even stopped going to a few of the PT/therapy appointments they scheduled once I started feeling a tiny bit better—I never bothered to reschedule, just couldn’t handle another waiting room.

Anyway, thanks for listening. Don’t tell Mom and Dad the details, okay? Just the PG‑13 version.

– Mike

Accident / symptom log – work and home

Started: 2025-12-15
Location focus: Travis Tech Solutions office, North Lamar, Austin, TX + home office at 1420 Willow Bend.

Purpose: keep track of when things are actually happening so I’m not guessing when I talk to Dr. Chen.

12/15 – Mild urgency at home, no accident.
12/22 – First full accident at Travis Tech (N. Lamar). Around 2:10 p.m., during a lull between tickets. Tried to make it to the bathroom, didn’t. Texted Elena Garcia, she quietly told me to log out and head home so the team wouldn’t notice.

01/09 – Second incident at the office. About 3:20 p.m., right after a client VPN call. Same drill: mess, panic, Elena G. found me near the break room and told me to “go home, don’t worry about it.” Spent the drive back praying traffic on I‑35 wouldn’t trap me again.

02/03 – Third time. Somewhere around 2:45 p.m. I didn’t even try to pretend it was fine; just messaged Elena and she said, “I’ll mark you as out sick, just get home.” That’s three times she’s sent me home early between 2:00 and 3:30 so far.

Since then I’ve mostly been working remote to avoid a repeat. Still, I log every near‑miss, every time I soil myself, whether it’s at the office or here. The unpredictability is the worst part—one day I’m okay, the next I’m afraid to sit through a 30‑minute Zoom.

Also need to remember: I’ve been scheduling extra in‑person therapy sessions when I can, partly because I’m more likely to actually do the exercises with someone watching than at home on my own. Probably why insurance keeps pushing back.

Update this log weekly.

Hi Patricia,

Figured I’d give you the long version since you’re the one always telling me to advocate for myself.

Health-wise, I’m still stuck in limbo. Lone Star keeps denying the Humira, saying I have to run through their preferred biologics first. Meanwhile, I’m juggling mesalamine and yet another prednisone taper. Between December 15 and February 20 I’ve missed around a dozen full workdays from pain and fatigue. Travis Tech has been understanding, but I can feel my luck running out.

The worst part was 01/11 at Sofia’s robotics club at Forest Creek Elementary. I had an accident in the hallway—like, full-on, in front of her. She was crying, I was mortified, and I’ve basically avoided all her school events for the rest of January. I still feel like I let her down.

Then there’s the money stuff. Because Lone Star won’t cover adalimumab, I’ve been rationing the mesalamine since early January—skipping doses on weekends—so we can scrape together $275 every month to send to St. David’s Round Rock Medical Center for Elena’s miscarriage bill from 09/19/24. It makes me angry that we’re still paying for that night while also fighting to get the meds I need now.

On top of that, I keep thinking about that 10/21/25 ER visit at Ascension Seton Williamson where they documented my 0.12 blood alcohol level. Dr. Flores warned it could haunt my Texas Mutual Life application, like another thing stacked against us.

Kids are otherwise okay. Sofia’s soccer practice near Old Settlers is still our Tuesday ritual when I’m up for it, and Lucas is learning to write his name without turning the “S” in “Sofia” into a snake.

Thanks for always pushing me to speak up, even when I don’t want to.

Love,
Michael

Title: GI follow-up – Dr. Chen (Crohn’s / meds / work impact)

Date: 2026-03-05
Time: 3:30–4:30 p.m.
Location: Austin Digestive Health Associates – Round Rock office
Attendees: Me, Dr. Lauren Chen

Notes to self:

1. Medication situation
- Update her that Lone Star Health PPO is still denying adalimumab (Humira) and insisting on step therapy with infliximab/ustekinumab.
- Explain I’ve been rationing mesalamine since early January—skipping weekend doses—to free up $275/month for Elena’s old St. David’s Round Rock Medical Center bill from the 09/19/24 miscarriage. Ask if she has any samples or patient assistance ideas so I’m not sabotaging my own treatment.

2. Symptom log
- Bring accident log from 12/15 onward. Mention the three soiling episodes at the Travis Tech North Lamar office where Elena Garcia quietly sent me home between 2:00–3:30 p.m. so coworkers wouldn’t notice.
- Talk about the 01/11 incident at Sofia’s robotics club at Forest Creek Elementary and how it’s made me avoid her school events.

3. Past ER visit / alcohol
- Ask whether the 10/21/25 Ascension Seton Williamson ER record showing BAL 0.12 is going to follow me around. Dr. Flores already warned it might affect Texas Mutual Life underwriting; want Chen’s take on how it looks in my GI chart.

4. Work/quality of life
- Explain missed workdays (12+ since mid‑December), fatigue, and nighttime prednisone insomnia.
- Ask honestly: what happens if Humira stays denied? Surgery timeline? Other biologics that don’t require infusion days?

Remember to bring: med list, accident log printout, insurance denial letters (if I can find them), water bottle, extra clothes in backpack just in case.

To: josh.miller@travistech.com
From: michael.rivera84@example.com
Subject: Schedule / appointments over the next few weeks

Hey Josh,

Wanted to give you a heads up about my availability in March so you’re not blindsided.

My Crohn’s has been a lot more unpredictable since December. You’ve probably noticed the pattern of me signing off early some afternoons—there were three days at the North Lamar office (12/22, 1/9, 2/3) where Elena G. quietly told me to head home between 2 and 3:30 because I’d had… let’s just call them “bathroom emergencies.” Since then I’ve been trying to stick to WFH as much as possible so it doesn’t disrupt the team.

I’ve got a follow‑up with my GI, Dr. Chen, on 3/5 at 3:30 p.m., and I may have additional imaging or infusion appointments depending on what she recommends. I’m still fighting with Lone Star over a medication (Humira) that she thinks would stabilize things, but until that’s sorted out I’m stuck on steroids and my old meds.

Just to be transparent: some of the PT/therapy sessions they set up last fall were more about convenience for me—having someone walk me through exercises so I didn’t have to do them on my own at home. Insurance pushed back on the extra visits, and when my symptoms eased up a bit I stopped going. In hindsight I probably should’ve stuck with them.
